pattern: Since every move costs either 0 or 1 health, the cheapest path in unsafe-cell count can be found with a double-ended queue instead of a general shortest-path priority queue.
hints: Reframe the problem: find the path from start to end that passes through the fewest possible unsafe (value 1) cells -- that minimum determines whether you survive.
Because each step's cost is only 0 or 1, a 0-1 BFS (push zero-cost moves to the front of a deque, cost-one moves to the back) finds that minimum without needing a full priority queue.
Track the running cost as health lost, starting from the top-left cell's own value; you can finish if that minimum cost, subtracted from health, leaves at least 1.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, reachable with exactly enough health
statement example, only path is through the center
all-safe grid, minimum possible health still succeeds
target cell itself is unsafe, health must account for it
a longer safe detour beats a short unsafe shortcut

stress.description: random grid with health scaled to the theoretical max needed
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = max(1, min(50, size))
    n = max(1, min(50, size))
    grid = [[rng.choice([0, 0, 1]) for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(m)]
    health = rng.randint(1, m + n)
    return [grid, health]
